item.title: - Depending on the king of mountains, the Himalayas, bhikkhus, great serpents grow their bodies and acquire strength. There, having grown their bodies and acquired…
item.description:
- Depending on the king of mountains, the Himalayas, bhikkhus, great serpents grow their bodies and acquire strength. There, having grown their bodies and acquired strength, they descend into small lakes. Having descended into small lakes, they descend into large lakes. Having descended into large lakes, they descend into small rivers. Having descended into small rivers, they descend into large rivers. Having descended into large rivers, they enter the great ocean, the sea. There, their bodies become large and vast.

So too, bhikkhus, a bhikkhu who depends on virtue, established in virtue, develops the seven factors of enlightenment and cultivates them, attains greatness and vastness in states.

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 46.1
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Bhikkhus, these two bright qualities protect the world. What two? Moral shame and moral dread. Bhikkhus, if these two bright qualities did not protect the…
item.description:
Bhikkhus, these two bright qualities protect the world. What two? Moral shame and moral dread. Bhikkhus, if these two bright qualities did not protect the world, then there would be no recognition of 'this is my mother,' 'this is my aunt,' 'this is my brother's wife,' 'this is my teacher's wife,' 'this is the wife of a respected elder'; the world would fall into promiscuity, like goats, sheep, poultry, pigs, dogs, and jackals.

But since, bhikkhus, these two bright qualities protect the world, there is recognition of 'this is my mother,' 'this is my aunt,' 'this is my brother's wife,' 'this is my teacher's wife,' 'this is the wife of a respected elder.'

Those for whom moral shame and dread
Are not always present
Have deviated from the bright root,
And go to birth and death.

But those for whom moral shame and dread
Are always well established,
Who are mature in the holy life,
Have peace, and an end of renewed existence.

From: Itivuttaka 42
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Sāriputta, do you know any white-robed householder whose conduct is protected by the five training rules, and who obtains without difficulty, without trouble, without effort…
item.description:
Sāriputta, do you know any white-robed householder whose conduct is protected by the five training rules, and who obtains without difficulty, without trouble, without effort the four higher states of mind, pleasant abidings in this very life, so that, if he wishes, he can declare of himself: 'I have destroyed hell, destroyed the animal realm, destroyed the ghost realm, destroyed the plane of misery, the bad destination, the lower realm. I am a stream-enterer, no longer subject to decline, assured of enlightenment.' Protected by which five training rules? Here, Sāriputta, a noble disciple abandons killing living beings, abandons taking what is not given, abandons sexual misconduct, abandons false speech, abandons intoxication with liquor and fermented drink. His conduct is protected by these five training rules. And which four higher states of mind, pleasant abidings in this very life, does he obtain without difficulty, without trouble, without effort? Here, Sāriputta, a noble disciple has unshakable serene confidence in the Buddha: 'The Blessed One is an Arahant, Perfectly Enlightened, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, well-gone, knower of the world, unsurpassed trainer of persons to be tamed, teacher of devas and humans, the Enlightened One, the Blessed One.' This is the first higher state of mind, a pleasant abiding in this very life, attained by purifying a mind not yet purified, by clarifying a mind not yet clarified. Furthermore, Sāriputta, a noble disciple has unshakable serene confidence in the Dhamma... Furthermore, Sāriputta, a noble disciple has unshakable serene confidence in the Saṅgha... Furthermore, Sāriputta, a noble disciple possesses the virtues loved by the noble ones, unbroken, unblemished, unstained, untainted, leading to liberation, praised by the wise, conducive to well-being, leading to concentration.

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 5.179
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Here, bhikkhus, a person is equal to oneself in virtue, concentration, and wisdom. Such a person, bhikkhus, should be associated with, dwelt with, served and…
item.description:
Here, bhikkhus, a person is equal to oneself in virtue, concentration, and wisdom. Such a person, bhikkhus, should be associated with, dwelt with, served and attended upon. Why is that? Thinking: 'We are both skilled in virtue, our conversation will be about virtue, which will be beneficial for both of us and lead to our well-being. We are both skilled in concentration... We are both skilled in wisdom, our conversation will be about wisdom, which will be beneficial for both of us and lead to our well-being.' Therefore, such a person should be associated with, dwelt with, served and attended upon.

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 3.26
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Bhikkhus, the world has been fully awakened to by the Tathāgata; the Tathāgata is detached from the world. Bhikkhus, the origin of the world has…
item.description:
Bhikkhus, the world has been fully awakened to by the Tathāgata; the Tathāgata is detached from the world. Bhikkhus, the origin of the world has been fully awakened to by the Tathāgata; the origin of the world has been abandoned by the Tathāgata. Bhikkhus, the cessation of the world has been fully awakened to by the Tathāgata; the cessation of the world has been realized by the Tathāgata. Bhikkhus, the path leading to the cessation of the world has been fully awakened to by the Tathāgata; the path leading to the cessation of the world has been developed by the Tathāgata. Bhikkhus, whatever in this world with its devas, Māras, and Brahmās, among the multitude of ascetics and brahmins, devas and humans, is seen, heard, sensed, cognized, attained, sought after, and examined by the mind — all that the Tathāgata has fully awakened to. Therefore he is called the Tathāgata. Bhikkhus, from the night when the Tathāgata fully awakens to unsurpassed perfect enlightenment until the night he attains final Nibbāna without remainder, whatever he speaks, utters, or declares — all that is just so and not otherwise. Therefore he is called the Tathāgata. Bhikkhus, the Tathāgata does what he speaks and speaks what he does. Because he does what he speaks and speaks what he does, therefore he is called the Tathāgata. Bhikkhus, in this world with its devas, Māras, and Brahmās, among the multitude of ascetics and brahmins, devas and humans, the Tathāgata is the conqueror, unconquered, all-seeing, all-knowing, supremely self-controlled. Therefore he is called the Tathāgata.

From: Itivuttaka 112
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: 'For their whole life, the Arahants have abandoned dancing, singing, music, and shows, do not adorn themselves with garlands, perfumes, ointments, or finery. Today, this…
item.description:
'For their whole life, the Arahants have abandoned dancing, singing, music, and shows, do not adorn themselves with garlands, perfumes, ointments, or finery. Today, this night and this day, I also abandon dancing, singing, music, and shows, do not adorn myself with garlands, perfumes, ointments, or finery. By this factor, following the example of the Arahants, I will observe the uposatha day.' This is the seventh factor accomplished.

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 9.18
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: The Blessed One saw the Venerable Khankhārevata sitting not far away, cross-legged, holding his body straight, contemplating his own purity through the overcoming of doubt.…
item.description:
The Blessed One saw the Venerable Khankhārevata sitting not far away, cross-legged, holding his body straight, contemplating his own purity through the overcoming of doubt. Then the Blessed One, having understood the meaning of this, on that occasion uttered this inspired utterance:

Whatever doubts there are
In this life or the next,
Not experienced by oneself
Or experienced by others,
A meditator abandons them all,
Completely.
Ardent, he lives the holy life.

From: Udāna 5.7
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Then, Bhāradvāja, I went to Āḷāra Kālāma and said: 'Friend Kālāma, is it to this extent that you have known for yourself, realized for yourself,…
item.description:
Then, Bhāradvāja, I went to Āḷāra Kālāma and said: 'Friend Kālāma, is it to this extent that you have known for yourself, realized for yourself, attained for yourself, and declared this Dhamma?' — 'Yes, friend, to this extent I have known, realized, attained, and declared this Dhamma.' — 'Friend, I too have known, realized, attained, and declare this Dhamma to this extent.' — 'It is a gain for us, friend, a great gain for us, that we see such a fellow celibate as the venerable one. The Dhamma that I have known, realized, attained, and declared — you too have known, realized, attained, and abide; the Dhamma that you have known, realized, attained, and abide — I too have known, realized, attained, and declared. The Dhamma I know, you know; the Dhamma you know, I know. As I am, so are you; as you are, so am I. Come now, friend, let the two of us take care of this assembly!' Thus, Bhāradvāja, Āḷāra Kālāma, who was my teacher, placed me, his disciple, on the same level as himself and honored me with supreme honor.

From: Majjhima Nikāya 100 – Saṅgāravasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Bhikkhus, there are these five benefits of walking meditation. What five? It endures long journeys; it endures exertion; it has few illnesses; it leads to…
item.description:
Bhikkhus, there are these five benefits of walking meditation. What five? It endures long journeys; it endures exertion; it has few illnesses; it leads to good digestion of food, drink, chewed, and tasted; the concentration attained while walking meditation lasts long. These, bhikkhus, are the five benefits of walking meditation.

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 5.29
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: The foolish, ignorant person Treats himself as an enemy, Doing evil deeds That bear bitter fruit. That deed is not well done Which, having been…
item.description:
The foolish, ignorant person
Treats himself as an enemy,
Doing evil deeds
That bear bitter fruit.

That deed is not well done
Which, having been done, one regrets,
And for which, with tearful face,
One weeps, receiving its result.

But that deed is well done
Which, having been done, one does not regret,
And for which, joyful and happy,
One receives its result.

The fool thinks it sweet
As long as evil has not ripened,
But when evil ripens,
The fool experiences suffering.

The evil deed done does not curdle immediately,
Like milk just milked;
Burning, it follows the fool,
Like fire covered with ash.

From: Dhammapada 60-75
📖 Read this sutta
